Transcript:
All right, we made it to the end of the
cadence and the infamous breakup email
is here.
Though unfairly criticized because of
poor breakup [music] emails of the past,
provided it's done correctly, the
breakup email is still one of the most
useful [music] tools you have. If your
breakup email sounds like a cringey
comedy routine, anyone remember [music]
those emails that asked you if you got
eaten by an alligator? Or if your email
[music] aggressively calls out your
buyer for not returning your emails, we
want to make some adjustments. The
breakup email is really [music] how you
close the loop with respect while
preserving your domain health and
leaving the buyer [music] thinking that
the content was helpful even when they
didn't respond. So, here's the framework
I want [music] you to use. First, a
helpful reset. Acknowledge that you've
sent a few notes, then make [music] the
lowest friction assumption you possibly
can make, which is timing. Not that they
are ignoring you, not that they are
busy,
>> [music]
>> not that they don't care, but timing.
That one line matters more than you
think because it does not poke at the
buyer's behavior. It simply acknowledges
reality.
>> [music]
>> The timing may not be right.
Second, continue to add value. This is
where you include a couple of resources
that match [music] the persona,
something that helps them do their job
better. A quick read, a link [music] to
a resource, or even a newsletter sign up
link. You do not need to go into the
level of detail here that you did in the
previous [music] standalone nurture
emails. Just tell them why you think
they might be interested in each one of
the resources [music] in one sentence
per resource. Think about two or four
resources here to leave them with.
And third, tell them what [music]
happens next. You, meaning the person
sending the email, are going to get in
touch later down the road and if they
need anything else in the meantime,
you're here.
And sign off. Subject line wise, keep it
simple and useful. Something like a few
resources you might find helpful.
Now, let's talk about social touches
because this is [music] where cadences
move from a sales reps greatest ally to
sales reps greatest headache.
As I mentioned before, [music] there is
a lot that goes into social selling
properly, which is well beyond the scope
of this video [music] series. But what I
want you to do is shift the focus of the
social touches away from ones that are
just checking a box. For example, we
might tell reps to like or [music]
comment on a post when that may not in
fact be useful. Instead, here are two
strategies I can offer. First, if your
cadences still have view the buyer's
profile as a step, [music] I'd recommend
you remove it and save the rep the
hassle. Most buyers aren't on the edge
of their seat, sadly, to see who looked
at their profile. And in fact, without
premium LinkedIn [music] subscriptions,
they'll only be able to see the last
five people who looked at their profile.
So, let's say your seller looks at
[music] 8:00 a.m. and then the buyer
does check their views, but not until
5:00 p.m. where [music] 12 other people
have looked at their profiles. Not
helpful, right? As for the second tip,
I'd recommend adding a social touch as
the fourth [music] touch in your
cadence. Encourage your rep to send a
connection request and [music] write
something that sounds like this.
Hi Leah, my name might look familiar as
I've [music] sent a few emails your way.
I'd be grateful for the chance to tell
you about how Sam Sales can be of
support to SalesLoft. If you're ever up
[music] for a chat, please let me know
and I'll send a calendar invite along
accordingly. Not only does this up the
chances that someone will go back to
read your emails, [music]
but the custom note will stand out in
their connection request inbox amongst
[music] a sea of blank connection
requests from other volume game sellers.
Again,
>> [music]
>> if you're a follower of mine or of Sam
Sales, you'll know that I'm not a big
advocate of cold calls, but I do accept
[music] that in some buyer personas,
calls still work very well.
And hopefully, [music] after this, so do
your emails. Now, let's briefly again
revisit cold calls cuz they may be an
important part of your cadence. If you
believe cold calls are effective for
you, I would again first [music]
encourage you to examine the data behind
the volume of calls. Things like how
many calls does it take to book a
meeting? What are the [music] percentage
of calls that are converting to
meetings? How often do those calls show
up? How often do those calls reschedule?
And then [music] look at the pipeline
volume and velocity from there. If
you're in an industry where you already
know that cold calls are effective,
[music]
one of the best times to do a cold call
is right after your first email.
For any of you who are out there and who
have already been working the phones,
[music] you know that most often you're
spending a lot of the day reaching
voicemails. This actually isn't a loss.
Leaving a voicemail that is a pitch is
unlikely to result in a callback.
[music] Leaving a voicemail instead that
points to the first email you just wrote
can be a great way to bring attention to
your email and get a response in that
channel instead of the phone.
Try a script like this. Hi Katie, this
is Sam McKenna calling from #samsales. I
sent you an email titled Where in the
World Is Carmen Sandiego and Tesla, but
also wanted to reach out here in case
the phone is easier. Excited to share
some ideas about how we can help Tesla
with this specific value prop.
Looking forward to talking more. Thanks.
If you find that more calls are the way
forward based on your data, then feel
free to [music] add more calls
throughout the cadence, making sure that
you leave no more than two voicemails.
Your second voicemail should sound
something like this. Hey Katie, my name
might sound familiar as I've emailed you
a few times, [music]
but this is Sam McKenna again and I'm
part of the team at #samsales. I would
so appreciate the opportunity to talk
with [music] you about how we can help
with specific value proposition. If
you'd be up for finding some time to
chat in the future, [music] let me know.
A few last thoughts before we wrap up
the series. First, grammar counts. If
writing isn't your strength, get a peer
[music] or a leader to review your
emails. In fact, this is one of the best
ways you can use AI to your advantage by
having it proofread your emails. Second,
keep building your nurture library.
Every time marketing [music] ships a new
asset, turn that into a nurture email
your reps can actually use. That is how
you scale and continue to provide
[music] your reps with valuable
materials written in the sales force.
And finally,
>> [music]
>> remember the point of the entire
cadence. You are selling interest in a
conversation, not trying to sell your
full [music] product suite over email.
And if you adopt the same mentality once
you've booked a meeting, which is to
help your buyers solve challenges rather
than simply pitch your products, [music]
I suspect you'll see a significant
uptick in your results.
